examples.title: Z-Buffer Algorithm
examples.description: Demonstrates the z-buffer (depth buffer) visibility algorithm used in most modern graphics hardware
examples.code:
ALGORITHM ZBufferRendering
INPUT: List of polygons, screen dimensions (width, height)
OUTPUT: Final colored image

BEGIN
 // Initialize buffers
 CREATE frameBuffer[width][height] // Color values
 CREATE zBuffer[width][height] // Depth values
 
 // Initialize z-buffer to maximum depth (far plane)
 FOR each pixel (x, y) IN screen DO
 zBuffer[x][y] ← INFINITY
 frameBuffer[x][y] ← backgroundColor
 END FOR
 
 // Process each polygon
 FOR each polygon IN polygonList DO
 // Rasterize polygon to fragments
 FOR each fragment (x, y, z, color) OF polygon DO
 // Depth test: only draw if fragment is closer
 IF z < zBuffer[x][y] THEN
 zBuffer[x][y] ← z
 frameBuffer[x][y] ← color
 END IF
 END FOR
 END FOR
 
 RETURN frameBuffer
END
examples.explanation:
1. Initialize both framebuffer (for colors) and z-buffer (for depths) to default values
2. For each polygon in the scene, rasterize it to generate pixel fragments
3. For each fragment, compare its depth (z) with the stored value in z-buffer
4. Only update the framebuffer if the new fragment is closer (smaller z value)
5. After processing all polygons, framebuffer contains correctly visible surfaces
6. Time complexity: O(n × p) where n = number of pixels, p = number of polygons
7. Space complexity: O(w × h) for both buffers

examples.title: Painter's Algorithm
examples.description: Demonstrates the painter's algorithm (depth sorting) for visible surface determination
examples.code:
ALGORITHM PaintersAlgorithm
INPUT: List of polygons with depth values
OUTPUT: Rendered image

BEGIN
 // Step 1: Calculate average depth for each polygon
 FOR each polygon IN polygonList DO
 polygon.avgDepth ← CalculateAverageZ(polygon.vertices)
 END FOR
 
 // Step 2: Sort polygons by depth (far to near)
 // Using bubble sort for demonstration
 FOR i ← 0 TO polygonList.length - 2 DO
 FOR j ← 0 TO polygonList.length - 2 - i DO
 IF polygonList[j].avgDepth < polygonList[j+1].avgDepth THEN
 SWAP polygonList[j] AND polygonList[j+1]
 END IF
 END FOR
 END FOR
 
 // Step 3: Render polygons from back to front
 FOR each polygon IN sortedPolygonList DO
 RasterizeAndDraw(polygon)
 // Each closer polygon overwrites previous
 END FOR
 
 RETURN renderedImage
END

FUNCTION CalculateAverageZ(vertices)
BEGIN
 sum ← 0
 FOR each vertex IN vertices DO
 sum ← sum + vertex.z
 END FOR
 RETURN sum / vertices.length
END
examples.explanation:
1. Calculate the average Z (depth) value for each polygon in the scene
2. Sort all polygons in descending order by their average depth (farthest first)
3. Render polygons from back to front - each new polygon draws on top of previous
4. Closer polygons naturally overwrite farther ones due to rendering order
5. Simpler than z-buffer but can fail with cyclic overlaps (A behind B, B behind C, C behind A)
6. Time complexity: O(n log n) for sorting, where n = number of polygons
7. Space complexity: O(n) for storing polygon depths
